content.text: Long-running AI agents depend on a chain of external services: LLM inference APIs, vector databases, tool endpoints, code execution sandboxes, and web search services. Each of those services can hang — responding far too slowly or not at all. Without explicit time limits, a single sluggish upstream call can hold an agent thread indefinitely, consuming resources and blocking user-facing requests.
is an upper bound placed on a single operation. It answers the question: "How long am I willing to wait for
before I give up?" If the call does not complete within the allowed window, the runtime cancels it and raises an exception.

is an absolute point in time by which an entire chain of work must finish. Unlike a per-call timeout, a deadline propagates through the entire call graph. If the orchestrator has 30 seconds to produce an answer and 12 seconds have already elapsed, then every remaining sub-task inherits a budget of at most 18 seconds — not the original per-call limit, whichever is smaller. Deadlines eliminate the subtle failure mode where each individual sub-call succeeds within its own timeout, but their cumulative duration exceeds the user's patience.

The two concepts compose: a deadline sets the ceiling for the total duration, while per-call timeouts provide fine-grained control within that budget. Both are essential in production agentic systems.

LLM inference is inherently slow: a single call to a frontier model can take anywhere from one second to well over a minute under load. Tool calls that hit external APIs can stall on DNS lookups, connection timeouts, or unresponsive servers. In a multi-step agent pipeline, these delays compound. A ten-step pipeline where each step occasionally takes 30 seconds instead of 2 seconds will regularly miss any user-facing latency SLA.

Without timeouts, agents fail in silent, dangerous ways. An HTTP connection that hangs at the TCP level will block the calling coroutine for the OS socket timeout — commonly two minutes or more. During this period the agent's concurrency slot is held, the user sees no feedback, and the system's observable throughput drops silently. Dashboards show low request rates but high resource usage.
Deadlines are particularly important in agentic systems because they eliminate

— the failure mode where each individual call respects its own timeout, but their sum exceeds the intended end-to-end budget. If five sequential calls each have a 15-second timeout, the cumulative maximum is 75 seconds even if the intended end-to-end budget is 30. A deadline prevents this accumulation.
Timed-out calls release threads, coroutines, and connection pool slots promptly rather than holding them until the OS socket timeout.
Timeouts cap the long tail of the latency distribution. The p99 of a pipeline with 30-second LLM timeouts cannot exceed 30 seconds per call, regardless of upstream slowness.
A user who receives a clear "request timed out" error within 30 seconds has a better experience than waiting three minutes before seeing a generic 500.

When an agent launches N parallel sub-tasks, a deadline ensures all of them are cancelled when the parent's budget expires — preventing orphaned tasks from continuing to make API calls and accumulating costs.
Timeout rates are a leading indicator of upstream service health. A spike in timeouts warns operators before a complete outage, giving time to activate fallback paths.

Retries require a per-attempt timeout to be meaningful. Without one, a single hung attempt blocks the entire retry budget. With one, a failed attempt is cancelled promptly and the retry starts within the remaining deadline.

Timeouts introduce a new class of error: "the operation may or may not have completed." When a call times out, the caller cannot know whether the remote service received the request. For idempotent operations this is safe to retry. For non-idempotent operations (writes, sends, state changes), a timed-out call requires an idempotency check before retrying.

Choosing the right timeout value is genuinely hard. A timeout that is too tight converts normal high-load slowness into unnecessary failures. A timeout that is too loose provides little protection. There is no universal correct value; it must be calibrated against empirical latency data from the target service.
A service that regularly responds in 8 seconds will be unnecessarily killed by a 5-second timeout at peak load. Calibrate timeouts against p99 latency, not median.
Every async call that respects a deadline must compute the remaining time and pass it through the call stack. Retrofitting this into an existing codebase requires touching every layer.
When a parent task is cancelled due to a deadline, Python's asyncio propagates cancellation to children. Functions that hold resources must catch
With a 10-second per-call timeout and 5 retries, the maximum elapsed time is 50 seconds — before adding backoff delays. Each retry attempt must use
In Python's asyncio, the standard timeout mechanism is
, which cancels the coroutine and raises
if it does not return within N seconds. Python 3.11+ adds
as a context manager, which is cleaner for blocks with multiple await points.
For HTTP calls, libraries like httpx expose separate

parameters. The connect timeout governs TCP handshake and TLS; the read timeout governs the gap between sending the request and receiving the first response byte. Both must be set — a read timeout alone still allows an infinite hang during connection establishment.
Deadlines work by maintaining a single
) and recomputing the per-call timeout at each call site:

evaluates to zero or negative, the deadline has already expired and the call should be rejected immediately without being issued. This avoids wasting API quota on calls that cannot contribute to a timely response.

In gRPC-based systems, deadline propagation is first-class: each call includes a deadline in its metadata, and the framework converts it to a per-call timeout. REST-based agentic systems must implement this propagation manually by passing a deadline context object and computing remaining time at each call site.

provides automatic cancellation propagation: when the parent scope is cancelled by a deadline, all child tasks in the group are cancelled atomically. This is safer than manually tracking task handles, which risks orphaned tasks continuing after the parent has given up.

attrs.alt: Timeout and deadline propagation in agentic AI systems: User Request sets a 30-second deadline, Orchestrator tracks it and computes per-call timeouts for Sub-tasks A/B/C which call LLM API, Tool Endpoint, and Vector DB respectively. A deadline context object flows through the call chain. Timeline at bottom shows budget allocation: sub-tasks 0-12s, synthesis 12-22s, formatting 22-28s, error buffer 28-30s.

content.text: arrives with an absolute deadline of
stores this as a monotonic timestamp and, before each downstream call, computes
. Sub-tasks A, B, and C run concurrently, each calling a different downstream service with a deadline-derived timeout.
If any sub-task's deadline-derived timeout expires, asyncio cancels it and raises

. The orchestrator catches this and either uses a partial result or applies a fallback. The timeline at the bottom shows how the 30-second budget is intentionally partitioned across pipeline phases, with a 2-second error buffer reserved for cleanup and response serialization.
Timeouts should be applied to every external call in an agentic system without exception. The practical question is not
Apply a read timeout of 30–120 seconds depending on model and expected output length. Streaming responses need a timeout applied between chunks, not just on the first byte.
Tool latency varies enormously. Web search: 1–5 seconds; code execution: up to 30 seconds; web browsing: potentially unbounded. Set explicit limits per tool type.
These should complete in under 2 seconds for well-maintained indexes. A 5-second timeout with a fallback to a cruder filter is appropriate.
When an orchestrator calls a sub-agent, pass a deadline-derived timeout. The sub-agent's entire execution budget must fit within the parent's remaining deadline.
Use deadlines when there is a user-facing SLA.
If users expect a response within 30 seconds, set a 30-second deadline at the entry point and propagate it through every hop. Per-call timeouts alone cannot enforce end-to-end latency.
Do not use a single timeout for all call types.
A 500ms timeout suitable for a cache lookup will prematurely kill a 30-second LLM generation. Each call type needs its own calibrated limit.
Set connect and read timeouts on every HTTP client.
When constructing httpx, aiohttp, or requests clients, pass explicit timeout objects. Never rely on OS defaults.
Pass a deadline timestamp (not a duration) through the call graph.
A duration shrinks as it is passed; a timestamp does not. Compute remaining time as
Compute per-call timeout as min(call_limit, remaining_deadline).
Never use a per-call timeout larger than the remaining deadline. If the remaining time is already smaller than the call limit, use the remaining time (or reject the call if already expired).
Wrap async calls with asyncio.wait_for or asyncio.timeout.
For coroutines that do not natively accept a timeout parameter,
provides cancellation. Python 3.11+ users can use
separately from other exceptions. Log them with the operation name, elapsed time, and configured timeout. Never silently swallow them.
Cancel sibling tasks on deadline expiry.
(Python 3.11+) for parallel sub-tasks; it cancels all siblings automatically when any task raises. With
Set the externally-advertised deadline 2–5 seconds before the internal deadline. This reserves time for cleanup, serialization, and network egress.
. Alert when the timeout rate for any operation exceeds 1% of total calls for more than 5 minutes.
Per-call Timeout with httpx and the Anthropic SDK

import asyncio
import httpx
import anthropic

# httpx.Timeout controls connect, read, write, and pool timeouts separately.
CONNECT_TIMEOUT = 5.0   # seconds to establish TCP + TLS
READ_TIMEOUT = 60.0     # seconds to receive the first byte of the response

async def call_llm_with_timeout(prompt: str) -> str:
    """
    Issue a single LLM call with explicit per-call timeouts.
    Raises anthropic.APITimeoutError on timeout.
    """
    client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic(
        api_key="sk-...",
        timeout=httpx.Timeout(
            connect=CONNECT_TIMEOUT,
            read=READ_TIMEOUT,
            write=5.0,
            pool=5.0,
        ),
    )
    try:
        response = await client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        )
        return response.content[0].text
    except anthropic.APITimeoutError:
        raise
    finally:
        await client.close()


async def main():
    try:
        text = await call_llm_with_timeout("Summarise the TCP/IP protocol in two sentences.")
        print(text)
    except anthropic.APITimeoutError as exc:
        print(f"LLM call timed out: {exc}")

asyncio.run(main())

import asyncio
import time
import anthropic
import httpx

TOTAL_BUDGET_S = 30.0       # end-to-end user-facing deadline
LLM_CALL_LIMIT_S = 20.0     # per-call LLM ceiling
TOOL_CALL_LIMIT_S = 5.0     # per-call tool ceiling
MIN_REMAINING_S = 1.5       # abort if less than this remains


async def call_llm(client: anthropic.AsyncAnthropic, prompt: str, deadline: float) -> str:
    remaining = deadline - time.monotonic()
    if remaining <= MIN_REMAINING_S:
        raise asyncio.TimeoutError("deadline too close for LLM call")
    per_call = min(LLM_CALL_LIMIT_S, remaining - MIN_REMAINING_S)
    response = await asyncio.wait_for(
        client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=512,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        ),
        timeout=per_call,
    )
    return response.content[0].text


async def call_tool(query: str, deadline: float) -> str:
    """Simulated tool call (web search) that respects the deadline."""
    remaining = deadline - time.monotonic()
    if remaining <= MIN_REMAINING_S:
        raise asyncio.TimeoutError("deadline too close for tool call")
    per_call = min(TOOL_CALL_LIMIT_S, remaining - MIN_REMAINING_S)
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=httpx.Timeout(per_call)) as http:
        response = await http.get(
            "https://api.example.com/search",
            params={"q": query},
        )
        response.raise_for_status()
        return response.json().get("summary", "")


async def agent_pipeline(question: str) -> str:
    """
    A two-step pipeline that enforces a single shared 30-second deadline.
    Both steps draw from the same time budget — not independent 30s windows.
    """
    deadline = time.monotonic() + TOTAL_BUDGET_S
    client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic(api_key="sk-...")
    try:
        # Step 1: gather context via tool (debits from the shared deadline)
        context = await call_tool(question, deadline)

        # Step 2: synthesise with LLM (uses whatever budget remains after step 1)
        prompt = f"Question: {question}\n\nContext: {context}\n\nAnswer concisely."
        return await call_llm(client, prompt, deadline)

    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - (deadline - TOTAL_BUDGET_S)
        return f"[Partial result — deadline exceeded after {elapsed:.1f}s]"
    finally:
        await client.close()


async def main():
    answer = await agent_pipeline("What is the current state of AI safety research?")
    print(answer)

asyncio.run(main())

A production customer-support agent receives a natural-language query, retrieves relevant documentation from a vector store, synthesises an answer with an LLM, and must respond within 20 seconds to satisfy a P95 SLA. Without deadline enforcement, the system behaves correctly under moderate load but fails at peak: vector store queries that typically complete in 300ms occasionally spike to 8 seconds; LLM calls that average 5 seconds occasionally reach 45 seconds during model capacity pressure.
The team instruments the pipeline with a 20-second deadline applied at the API gateway and propagated as a
argument through every call. The retrieval layer computes
. When either fires, the handler returns a graceful degradation response rather than a 500.

import asyncio
import time
import anthropic

DEADLINE_S = 20.0
DB_TIMEOUT_S = 3.0
LLM_TIMEOUT_S = 15.0
MIN_REMAINING_S = 1.5

FALLBACK = {
    "billing": "For billing questions, please contact support@example.com.",
    "default": "I'm unable to provide a personalised answer right now. Please try again shortly.",
}


def pick_fallback(query: str) -> str:
    if "billing" in query.lower() or "invoice" in query.lower():
        return FALLBACK["billing"]
    return FALLBACK["default"]


async def retrieve_docs(query: str, deadline: float) -> list[str]:
    remaining = deadline - time.monotonic()
    if remaining < MIN_REMAINING_S:
        raise asyncio.TimeoutError("deadline expired before DB retrieval")
    timeout = min(DB_TIMEOUT_S, remaining - MIN_REMAINING_S)
    # Simulated async DB call — replace with real vector store client:
    await asyncio.wait_for(asyncio.sleep(0.3), timeout=timeout)
    return [
        "Refund policy: returns accepted within 30 days.",
        "Billing cycles reset on the 1st of each month.",
    ]


async def synthesise(
    client: anthropic.AsyncAnthropic,
    query: str,
    docs: list[str],
    deadline: float,
) -> str:
    remaining = deadline - time.monotonic()
    if remaining < MIN_REMAINING_S:
        raise asyncio.TimeoutError("deadline expired before LLM call")
    timeout = min(LLM_TIMEOUT_S, remaining - MIN_REMAINING_S)
    context = "\n".join(docs)
    response = await asyncio.wait_for(
        client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=300,
            messages=[{
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"Using only the documentation below, answer the customer question concisely.\n"
                    f"\nDocumentation:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {query}"
                ),
            }],
        ),
        timeout=timeout,
    )
    return response.content[0].text


async def handle_query(query: str) -> str:
    deadline = time.monotonic() + DEADLINE_S
    client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic(api_key="sk-...")
    try:
        docs = await retrieve_docs(query, deadline)
        return await synthesise(client, query, docs, deadline)
    except asyncio.TimeoutError as exc:
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - (deadline - DEADLINE_S)
        print(f"[WARN] query timed out after {elapsed:.2f}s: {exc}")
        return pick_fallback(query)
    finally:
        await client.close()


async def main():
    for q in ["What is your refund policy?", "When does my billing cycle reset?"]:
        ans = await handle_query(q)
        print(f"Q: {q}\nA: {ans}\n")

asyncio.run(main())

After deploying this pattern, fewer than 0.2% of requests activate the fallback handler under normal load. During a 90-second partial LLM capacity event, the fallback rate spikes to 15%, but all users receive an actionable response within 20 seconds rather than experiencing a 60-second hang followed by a 500 error. The timeout telemetry becomes a leading indicator for LLM capacity health.
Setting Only a Read Timeout (Ignoring Connect Timeout)

A read timeout applies after the connection is established. If the upstream server is unreachable (DNS failure, firewall drop, network partition), the TCP handshake hangs for the OS default — often two minutes or more. Always set both:

import httpx

# WRONG: no timeout at all — the httpx default in older versions
client_wrong = httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=None)

# CORRECT: explicit per-operation timeouts
client_correct = httpx.AsyncClient(
    timeout=httpx.Timeout(
        connect=5.0,   # TCP + TLS handshake
        read=30.0,     # first byte of response
        write=5.0,     # sending request body
        pool=2.0,      # waiting for a connection pool slot
    )
)
Not Propagating the Deadline Through the Call Chain
The most common architectural mistake is computing a deadline at the entry point and then issuing each sub-call with its own independent timeout, without checking how much time remains:

import asyncio
import time

async def wrong_approach(deadline: float):
    # Each call uses its own 10s timeout, independent of the deadline.
    # Two slow calls can total 18s — exceeding a 15s deadline.
    result_a = await asyncio.wait_for(slow_call_a(), timeout=10.0)
    result_b = await asyncio.wait_for(slow_call_b(), timeout=10.0)
    return result_a, result_b

async def correct_approach(deadline: float):
    BUFFER = 1.0
    CALL_LIMIT = 10.0

    remaining = deadline - time.monotonic()
    if remaining < BUFFER:
        raise asyncio.TimeoutError("deadline expired before call A")
    result_a = await asyncio.wait_for(slow_call_a(), timeout=min(CALL_LIMIT, remaining - BUFFER))

    remaining = deadline - time.monotonic()
    if remaining < BUFFER:
        raise asyncio.TimeoutError("deadline expired before call B")
    result_b = await asyncio.wait_for(slow_call_b(), timeout=min(CALL_LIMIT, remaining - BUFFER))

    return result_a, result_b
Swallowing TimeoutError Instead of Handling It
) can jump backward or forward during NTP adjustments or VM migrations. Deadline arithmetic must use
, which never jumps backward. A deadline computed with
can appear to expire instantly if the clock steps forward.
Calibrate against p99 latency, not median.
Set timeouts to 2–3× the p99 latency observed under realistic load. A p99 of 8 seconds for LLM calls suggests a timeout of 20–25 seconds.
Check remaining budget before every call.
, skip the call and apply the fallback immediately rather than wasting API quota on a call that cannot produce a timely result.
Set the internally-enforced deadline 2–5 seconds before the externally-advertised SLA. This reserves time for response serialization, error handling, and network egress.
for parallel sub-tasks. TaskGroup cancels all siblings automatically when one raises, preventing orphaned tasks.
Log every timeout as a structured event.
Include operation name, configured timeout, and actual elapsed time. Aggregate as a metric tagged by operation type and alert on rates exceeding 1%.
Write tests that inject artificial slowness (
in a mock) to verify that timeout exceptions are caught and converted to the correct fallback. Untested timeout handlers invariably have bugs.
Document the rationale for each timeout value.
Add a comment: "30s — p99 of claude-opus-4-5 under 80% load is ~18s; 30s gives headroom for capacity spikes." Undocumented magic numbers are changed incorrectly during maintenance.
Timeouts have near-zero overhead on the happy path. The asyncio event loop checks the timeout precisely when the awaited coroutine yields, adding one comparison per await point. The dominant cost is
when a timeout fires: asyncio traverses the task tree and schedules cancellation callbacks. For a flat task graph this takes microseconds; for a wide fan-out tree it may take a few milliseconds.
The more significant performance effect is

. A pipeline without timeouts that has 0.5% of LLM calls hanging for 120 seconds will see its p99.5 dominated by those hangs. With a 30-second timeout, the p99.5 is bounded at 30 seconds. Those 0.5% of calls now fail quickly rather than eventually succeeding — correct behaviour if they are already beyond the user's patience.
Under high concurrency, if many agents each hold a connection for up to 30 seconds, the pool must be sized for peak concurrent connections. A pool that is too small causes
errors that appear before the read timeout fires. Monitor pool saturation separately.

For LLM streaming calls, the read timeout applies between successive chunks, not just on the first byte. If the model stalls mid-stream, the read timeout must fire between chunks. Most async HTTP clients support this natively.
When implementing retry-with-backoff inside an async pipeline, use
(blocks the event loop thread, freezing all concurrent coroutines during the delay).
Recommended async HTTP client for agents; fine-grained per-phase control
Alternative async HTTP; total= enforces end-to-end for the full request
timeout= on client or per-call; raises APITimeoutError
Wraps httpx; accepts httpx.Timeout or float
Backend-agnostic; works with asyncio and trio
Wall-clock deadline on the total retry loop
First-class propagation; server receives remaining time in context
Always set; necessary but not sufficient for SLA enforcement
Use for non-HTTP async calls; tool wrappers; internal coroutines
Best practice for any user-facing agentic pipeline with a latency SLA
Complements timeouts; stops calls to persistently slow services
Preferred for gRPC-based agent meshes; REST requires manual propagation
Best for multi-hour workflows; overkill for single HTTP pipelines
Timeouts and circuit breakers are complementary. Use
to bound individual call duration; use a
to stop issuing calls to a service that has been consistently timing out for the last N minutes. Together they provide both per-call and sustained-failure protection.
Denial of service via slow endpoints.

An agent that calls an untrusted tool endpoint without a timeout can be held open indefinitely by a malicious server. Always apply aggressive timeouts (5 seconds or less) on calls to externally-supplied tool endpoints.
When a timeout fires, the cancelled task must release file handles, database transactions, and connection slots. Use
to ensure cleanup runs even when cancelled via
If a pipeline writes intermediate results to a database and is cancelled mid-write, the row may be corrupt. Use database transactions so that either the full write commits or nothing does.
Do not log sensitive data in timeout callbacks.
Timeout exception objects can contain request bodies, prompt text, or API keys. Log only the status code, operation name, and elapsed time — not the full exception body.
Avoid too-tight timeouts causing retry storms.

If a timeout is set below the p99 latency of the target service, more than 1% of calls will time out under normal load. If callers retry immediately, the service receives amplified load, pushing more requests above p99 — a self-reinforcing failure cascade. Set timeouts above the p99 and pair with retry budgets, not unbounded retries.
Monitor timeout-rate spikes as a security indicator.

A sudden spike in timeout rates on a specific tool endpoint can indicate the endpoint has been compromised and is intentionally delaying responses. Alert on statistically anomalous rates per endpoint.
bounds an entire pipeline. Both are required — timeouts alone cannot enforce end-to-end latency guarantees.
at each call site. Reject calls immediately if the remaining budget is already below a minimum safety threshold.
timeouts on HTTP clients. A read timeout alone still allows the TCP handshake to hang for OS defaults (often 2+ minutes).
for all deadline arithmetic. Wall-clock time (
) can step backward during NTP adjustments, causing deadlines to expire instantly or never.
in functions that hold resources and perform cleanup in
blocks. Uncancelled child tasks cause resource leaks.

Calibrate timeout values against the p99 latency of the target service under realistic load — not the median. Set the timeout at 2–3× the p99 to avoid false timeouts while still bounding tail latency.

Instrument every timeout as a structured metric. A spike in timeout rates is a leading indicator of upstream degradation. Alert when the rate for any operation type exceeds 1% sustained for more than 5 minutes.

Pair timeouts with graceful degradation: return a cached result, a reduced-quality answer, or a clear actionable error when the deadline fires. Users tolerate bounded waits with clear feedback far better than unbounded hangs.
